sourceMovie.title: Stardust
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Stardust works as a adventure movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The adventure and fantasy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Maleficent
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Stardust who respond to its adventure will find Maleficent (2014) immediately familiar. Both films share a fantasy sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Aladdin
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Stardust fans keep landing on Aladdin (2019) — the adventure is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: The Little Mermaid
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Little Mermaid draws from the same adventure well as Stardust, channeling fantasy in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2023 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children (2016) captures the adventure appeal of Stardust without simply imitating it. The fantasy streak running through both films is what puts Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Shrek Forever After
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The adventure appeal of Stardust — that funny edge, the fantasy — translates cleanly into Shrek Forever After (2010). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Cinderella
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Cinderella (2015) clearly understood what makes fantasy work — the same quality that keeps Stardust rewatchable. Expect the same bittersweet rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Chronicles of Narnia: Prince Caspian
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Chronicles of Narnia: Prince Caspian (2008) is the kind of film Stardust fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The adventure overlap is real, but it's the uplifting emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Enchanted
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Enchanted shares Stardust's gift for fantasy — the 2007 film delivers romance without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Sleeping Beauty
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Stardust compelling — the fantasy, the romance — shows up in Sleeping Beauty (1959) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that uplifting storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Where the Wild Things Are
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The adventure energy of Stardust is alive in Where the Wild Things Are (2009), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a uplifting payoff that rivals the original pick.

faq.question: What should I watch after Stardust?
faq.answer: Try Maleficent, Aladdin, The Little Mermaid—each shares DNA with Stardust while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Stardust?
faq.answer: Stardust (2007) is best described as Adventure, Fantasy, Romance, Family. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Stardust worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this adventure lane.
